votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the cyclic shift check and verifies if any cyclic shift of the array is sorted, matching the Coq specification.

The Python code correctly checks whether there exists a cyclic shift of arr that is sorted. The is_cyclic_shift function checks all rotations, and move_one_ball_spec checks if result equals True iff some cyclic shift of arr is sorted (using <= for comparison, matching Z.le). The biconditional in Coq (result = true <-> ...) is captured by checking bool(result) == exists_sorted_shift. Both implementations agree on concrete inputs.

The Python code correctly checks if there exists a cyclic shift of arr that is sorted. The is_cyclic_shift function checks all possible rotations, and move_one_ball_spec checks if result equals whether such a sorted cyclic shift exists. The Coq spec says result=true iff there exists a sorted cyclic shift, which matches the Python logic. The Python handles the empty list case correctly (empty list is trivially sorted, and rotating by 0 gives the same list).

The Python code correctly checks if there exists a cyclic shift of arr that is sorted. is_cyclic_shift checks all rotations by iterating through split points. move_one_ball_spec checks if result equals True iff there exists a sorted cyclic shift, which matches the Coq biconditional. The iteration range len(arr)+1 covers all rotations (including identity at i=0 and i=len(arr) which are equivalent). The sorted check using Z.le corresponds to <= in Python.

Python enumerates all rotations arr[i:]+arr[:i] (exactly the Coq is_cyclic_shift cases) and checks nondecreasing order with <=, matching Sorted Z.le. The spec function returns True iff result equals the existence of a sorted rotation, aligning with the Coq iff. Edge cases like empty lists are handled consistently.
Python enumerates all rotations of the list and checks nondecreasing order, matching Coq’s is_cyclic_shift and Sorted Z.le; the spec returns whether result is true iff such a rotation exists.

Python enumerates all cyclic rotations (including duplicates) and checks nondecreasing order with <=, matching Coq’s is_cyclic_shift and Sorted Z.le. It returns whether result equals the existence of a sorted rotation. Length check is consistent; edge cases like empty/singleton lists align.
